@FND and I had a long conversation about this in IRC where were apparently completely agreeing with one another while completely misunderstanding one another. Typical.

Anyway, the conclusion from that is that there are two issues here:

* When passing in a file to `gabbi-run` the path to that file needs to become the `test_directory` that is provided to `test_suite_from_dict`. That's relatively easy to make happen and gets things at least working sensibly.
* More difficult is the test data in a child or adjacent directory. The `basename` call kills this. We could change that such that the path can be in `test_directory` or any descendants, but we wouldn't want to change it to allow anything outside of that tree as that would loosen the desirable constraint of not being able to get at data outside of the suite's context.

We can probably useful fix this in two steps: resolve the easy problem, see how that feels, and then decide how best to fix the harder problem.

Thoughts?

> error "failed to fetch file at path \"main\""

This means gdb was not able to determine which file was associated with the function `main`, so it told gdbgui the path was `main`. gdbgui then complains that it can't find it (because there isn't a file named "main", which is true).

Are you debugging with rust or go? You might want to disable the "auto add breakpoint to main" since gdb doesn't do a good job determining files from function names with those languages.

Flower shows all config options received from celery without modification.

In general, Celery removes passwords before replying to conf requests like in case of `BROKER_URL`. But Celery once is a third party library and `ONCE_REDIS_URL` is not a standard Celery option. I'd suggest to open a Celery issue to prevent sending non standard options or at least implement some mechanism to filter sensitive information.

As a workaround, you can disable conf responses from celery or override the default implemantation.

If I understand correctly, the [current active python should be searched for first](https://github.com/Kitware/CMake/blob/master/Modules/FindPythonInterp.cmake#L93), but `Python_ADDITIONAL_VERSIONS` overrides this and those additional pythons are searched for first (in order) instead.

So adding 3 to the end of `Python_ADDITIONAL_VERSIONS` won't solve this issue. It will still find `python2` instead of the active python.

`Python_ADDITIONAL_VERSIONS` appears to only be necessary when the active python is *not* the python that GLAD should use.

Btw, Sequential() usually expects to get a list instead of dictionary, i.e. `aug_seq = iaa.Sequential([iaa.Affine(rotate=(-40, 40))])` instead of `aug_seq = iaa.Sequential({iaa.Affine(rotate=(-40, 40))})`. Shouldn't cause an issue here, but when there are multiple augmenters in Sequential it might lead to them being executed in an unexpected order as standard dictionaries to not have a clearly determined order.

It occurs to me that curio *could* potentially get *much* fancier about its scheduler: e.g. it could spawn a worker thread to do the actual watching for I/O and calculate which task ought to be running at each time (taking into account an up-to-date view of I/O readiness, statistics on different tasks, etc.). And set a flag whenever a task has overstayed its welcome (i.e., when it would have been pre-empted in a non-cooperative system). And then when we hit a pre-emption point like `sock.recv`, all the main thread would have to do is quickly check this flag, and only yield if its set.

Closing this.   However, might modify behavior of spawn() back to a child-first arrangement.  Ran into something recently where that would have been advantageous.   Also, it's documented as doing this.

Three things:

The 'mu' symbol may not work in all environments. It should be fine in more modern systems where unicode is ubiquitous, but we probably can't make that guarantee for older systems.

Secondly:

Using 'u' as a substitue for 'micro' is pretty standard among the engineers here at Stanford, so it isn't that unusual...

Thirdly:

Pint coded in such a way as to give the end user a lot of control. If you want to change the 'u' to a 'µ', edit [this line](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/8edd45f35599071fac19f784619cc1871baa6c7e/pint/default_en.txt#L18) in your `default_en.txt` file.

Also, there's some work in the pipeline to fix this with `__numpy_ufunc__`/`__array_ufunc`.

What are your thoughts on my proposed workaround above?

@Manasvibhatt297 just a quick note on some etiquette and suggestions.

As I'm sure you know, everyone here is working on will in their free time, in addition to their regular work and such.  Because of that, it's not possible for folks to respond right away.  It's also considered pretty rude to re-post the same issue in multiple places, and directly ping unrelated people.  We're all stretched and stressed, and saying that it's urgent is more likely to make folks look away than stop to help.

Broadly, here's the kind of request and/or bug report that is going to get you the best responses, both here and everywhere across the internet.

1. **Clearly document your complete configuration**, what you've done, and how you got to this point.
2. **Document your problem**, including both what you see happening, and what you expect to be happening.  By the end of steps 1 and 2, anyone who comes across your issue should be able to reproduce it without talking to you.
3. **Do your homework, and communicate that**.  Open-source code is different than off-the-shelf stuff in that you can read the code and understand what's happening.  For a lot of us, this process of figuring out what was going wrong in our setup is a big part of how we learned to code in the first place.  
At the least, showing that you're looking through a package, have googled everything you can, and have made a serious effort will help your chances that someone will take the time to get things fixed.  The more lifting you do, the less it puts on the stranger who's helping you. The easier you can make it for them, the more likely they are to help.
4. **Spend time on formatting**.  This follows the above point, but specific to how you document and communicate your issue.  Take a bit of time to understand github markdown, put your code and output in styled fences, and edit your issues to clearly communicate the above, instead of adding a ton of comments. Include the configuration, what steps were taken, what was supposed to happen, and what did.
5. **Be kind.**  It's the [core principle](http://skoczen.github.io/will/improve/#our-culture) of our culture, and it holds up across the internet.  Appreciate that people are spending the time they could be spending with their families and friends to help out strangers on the internet, and make something useful.  Showing a little appreciation and acknowledgement goes a long way.
6. **Be patient.**  Everyone is busy, and we're all triaging to see stuff we can fit into tiny windows of time throughout our days.   If there's an issue that has 1-5 covered, it's more likely to get attention sooner - but even if not, generally, someone will get to it eventually.  Hang in there.

In this case, I got and replied to your first issue, didn't see any of 1-5 done there, and back-burnered it.  You responded by opening a second issue, and commenting on another closed issue and pinging several people who weren't involved.  None of those things are helping you get an answer sooner, and they're making extra work  (and hurting your reputation) along the way.

My guess, not knowing you, is that you're pretty new to github and maybe development.  That's cool - we've all been there.   I'd recommend you read around on [stack overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30486628/adding-issue-to-github-what-is-etiquette) or similar sites to understand the cultural conventions for sites like github, and open-source projects in general.  It's learning that will serve you well, throughout your journey.

Finally, on your actual issue, I'm keeping this issue open, closing your extra ones, and redirecting the other pings you've made here. 

I understand that your will isn't responding.  There are also thousands of people using will successfully, so it's very likely that the problem is in your setup.

Please document your setup (OS, python version, pip libraries installed, which variables you're setting in your virtualenv, which you're setting in `config.py`, and any steps to reproduce, and things you've already tried in getting it fixed.)  Do 1-4 on this issue, and it's going to make it much, much faster to get whatever you're running into resolved.

Thanks for listening, and for being a part of the will community!

More soon!

Thanks for the thorough explanation, @EikeDehling !

> I'm fine with your naming suggestion, only remark is: There is no cross-validation going on in the algorithm as far as i can see? I don't have a strong opinion on this naming though. Happy to go with your choice.

I agree with you, it's not really a k-fold cross-validation going on here, but just a k-fold like sampling. I think we chose that name because we had the `StackingClassifier` and they called in Stacking classification with k-fold cross-validation in 

- Tang, J., S. Alelyani, and H. Liu. "Data Classification: Algorithms and Applications." Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery Series, CRC Press (2015): pp. 498-500.

 I also don't have a strong preference, but maybe we should stick to `StackingCVRegressor` for simplicity/analogy.

> I'm not a theoretical ML expert, so i'm not sure what approach works better. Perhaps the ideas from StackingCVClassifier would even be an improvement? I would be happy to run some experiments and then code the best performing version.

Is this based on some paper in literature, or is this a "new" algorithm you came up with during experimentation? If you'd run some experiments to compare both approaches, that would be great, but don't worry about it if it's too much work (however, that could also be an interesting study for a paper if this hasn't been done before :)).

> Now the difference starts, the N instances of the base model are kept, and used during predicting. There it does N predictions, one with each instance of the base model, and averages them as input for the second level model. Then the second level model predicts final output.

Say we have n=10 samples in the dataset and k=5 1st-level regressors. Then, during prediction, the 1st level regressors first produce n*k=50 predictions, which are then averaged over *k* so that the 2nd level regressor gets *n* values instead of all 50 values? And the final prediction is the prediction of the 2nd level regressor on those *n* values?  I think the only difference to the StackingCVClassifier would be the averaging part. I.e., if you would stack the predictions instead of averaging them prior to giving them to the 2nd-level classifier, the algorithms would be identical except classification vs regression.

If that's indeed the case, I'd suggest to maybe toggle the averaging by a parameters. For example,

    StackingCVRegressor(, ..., use_averaged_predictions_in_secondary)

or sth like that. The default could then be the setting (`True`/`False`) that would work better in practice.

PS: There's another parameter in the `StackingCVClassifier` that could be transferred to the StackingCVRegressor`:

- `use_features_in_secondary : bool (default: False)`

We have a list L which contains say (features_list, metric) and say the index is the number of features within features_list.

Current subset {0, 1, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12} 
L[9] = ((0, 1, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12), L9_{metric})
Step1: Backw {0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12} [eliminated 6]
L[8] = ((0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12), L8_{metric})
Step2: Incl {0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12} [included 11]
Now, in order to add 11, then the metric of {0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12} must be > than the metric for (0, 1, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12). Hence, at this point we have L[8] and L[9] and we know that {0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12} is better than (0, 1, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12),.

Step1: Backw {0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12} [eliminated 11]
For this to happen, we must assume that by removing any is at least as bad/good as removing 11 and it just so happens for 11 to be randomly selected. A metric is assigned to (0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12). If this is better than what the metric we already have in L[8][1] then we substitute. Nevertheless, we have the same sequence of features stored and hence no substitution happens (not that it matters for what we are trying to address).

Step2: Incl {0, 1, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12} [included 6]
Now, here we know that the algorithm cannot include 6 as "the metric of {0, 1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12} must be > than the metric for (0, 1, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12)" and hence, this inclusion will not happen due to:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7026980/30156140-83a7a964-93c7-11e7-8944-07709be68814.png)

Which is not satisfied and as such Step 2 does not happen. Note that, if 6 was the best candidate then no other attribute was any better and as such this step will inherently be a removal and not an inclusion.

In my use case where I am modifying an entry I receive the writer via `entry.entry_writable()`. When removing the "description" attribute from an entry I noticed that when accessing the entry it didn't raise LDAPCursorError. Here is my work in progress implementation with workaround and a test.

```
    def modify_site(self, entry, operation=LdapFacadeBase.MODIFY_OVERWRITE, \
                    ou=None, description=None):
        # Check if entry is an organizationalUnit and
        # belong to expected ldap branch.
        self._validate_site_object(entry)

        # Validate format of ou.
        if ou: self._validate_ou_string(ou)

        # Create a writer for modifying entry.
        writer = entry.entry_writable()
        if ou:
            if operation == self.MODIFY_OVERWRITE:
                writer.ou = ou
            elif operation == self.MODIFY_ADD:
                writer.ou += ou
            elif operation == self.MODIFY_REMOVE:
                writer.ou -= ou
        if description:
            if operation == self.MODIFY_OVERWRITE:
                writer.description = description
            elif operation == self.MODIFY_ADD:
                writer.description += description
            elif operation == self.MODIFY_REMOVE:
                writer.description -= description
        # Store changes, refresh=True (default) updates Entry object from ldap.
        if writer.entry_commit_changes(refresh=True):
            # Original entry does not raise
            # ldap3.core.exceptions.LDAPCursorError anymore
            # if a property is removed completely.
            if operation == self.MODIFY_REMOVE:
                return self.get_site(entry.ou.values[0]) # New entry from ldap.
            return entry # Entry object gets updated automatically on commit.
        if writer.entry_changes:
            error_str = (
                "Could not commit changes \"{changes}\". "
                "Last connection error was \"{connerror}\"."
            )
            raise Exception(error_str.format(
                changes=writer.entry_changes,
                connerror=self.get_connection().last_error
            ))
        return entry # No changes.
```

I had a look and the SHA3 implementation explains why my "fix" is actually buggy:

```c
    /* Once a lock exists all code paths must be synchronized. We have to
     * release the GIL even for small buffers as acquiring the lock may take
     * an unlimited amount of time when another thread updates this object
     * with lots of data. */
```

The race condition I tried to introduce is where you start a long-running `update()` and then release the GIL, and an `update()` for a small buffer comes in and the two `update()` collide. Oops. 🤦

In any case, I agree that matching the hashlib API is a more important goal than an improved implementation. So rather than trying to fix this PR so that it at least raises an exception if a lock cannot be allocated, I'll close it ( and #17) and see about discussing a more flexible HASHLIB_GIL_MINSIZE with the cpython developers.

Any `docopt`-alike implementation is very fast for development, but it has limitations that can't be fixed:
You can't allow to implement addiotional commands/options. This will block plugin author from adding service-specific commands (i.e. for service with locally-accessible repositories something like `git --git-dir=/home/git/gogs-repositories/user/repo.git remote ...`).

> Re: default action: Can you explain what the use case/workflow for not adding a newly created repo as a remote is?

Because when you do `git X create foo/bar` you're might be doing it in advance for a future code you're about to push, and you don't want to mess up another repository you might be in. And then you'd end up having repositories of two projects mixed together in a single `.git/config`, not nice.

As I considered that if you actually aimed to add the default upstream in current repository but forgot to do so, running `git lab add group/repo gitlab` wouldn't be too much of a hassle, I thought it would be a sane default to not mutate current repository when doing `create`.

Just found this as well :)
Though I would have used get_socket, to keep the interface.

`self.remote_conn.get_socket().sendall(telnetlib.IAC + telnetlib.NOP)`

In this context the SSH socket would be still open (i.e. there is still a TCP session open between the two devices), but the SSH channel has closed at least one way from network device to server running Netmiko.

We will raise a specific error to notify things like Salt that they need to restart the SSH session (probably implies restarting a completely new SSH connection as Netmiko doesn't have any way of restarting a new channel and good chance most network devices would fail if we tried this anyways).

So then raises the question if this happens of whether we should gracefully close the SSH session (i.e. disconnect() ). Which I would think would be yes.

@aliutkus, I really don't think stereo signals should be expected. In particular for digital speech enhancement/denoising tasks a single-channel setting is still common. There's a strong use case for mono recordings in real world applications in terms of data bandwidth and so on, particularly for cellphones. Even the iPhone defaults to recording mono audio files, in fact.

For example [(source)](https://hal.inria.fr/hal-01163493/document):
>[...]we provide a systematic comparison of single-channel and two-channel methods, showing that RNN-based single-channel enhancement can yield a recognition performance that is on par with the previous best two-channel system, and at the same time is complementary to two-channel pre-processing.

However, in my experience bss_eval_sources is a bit easy to fool and you could get pretty decent SDR by just bandpassing the mixture, for example. This is mostly a problem due to global SDR being used, I believe. If there were local measures in mir_eval for estimating a lower bound (e.g. what's _the worst_ source-to-distortion ratio we'd expect to get in a separation?)  it would not be as much of a problem. So, basically just add boxplots for showing SDR, SIR, SAR variance like in the SiSEC 2015 paper, and I'd be pretty happy. :tada:

Sorry, I haven't been on top of GitHub notifications.

> there were a lot of issues with these tables in the past

My issue is entirely with the `pyc` files, which result from the way the tables are built. Not with the tables.

After that, I'm going to reply out of order, because I think that makes a little more sense.

> Currently `_build_tables` is run during installation to generate new `.py` files that weren't shipped. I'm not 100% sure pip actually does that, since this is a custom step that happens after the actual installation.

Yeah, you can't do anything during installation in a wheel world, everything should be shipped in the wheel. However, you *are* running `_build_tables` during `sdist` which means those `.py` files are shipped in source tarballs. And running them during `install` which gets them into wheels. I think that is all correct. What I'm questioning is including `.pyc` files, which is a side-effect of the way `_build_tables` is being run.

> The problem is, if there's no .pyc in the install directory, the .pyc is created locally where users of pycparser live

Are you sure about that? That sounds completely wrong. `.pyc` files should only be created next to their `.py` source, in Python 2, or in the neighbouring `__pycache__` directory in Python 3. If the user doesn't have permission to write the `.pyc` files in those places, they simply don't get written, and Python doesn't complain. It does affect import performance, but that's it.

> Therefore, at the very least I wouldn't run with -B during install, perhaps only during sdist.

I think that'd be wrong. Then you'd ship `.pyc` files in wheels. But the wheels target *all* versions of Python, and the `.pyc` files don't.

Always open to supporting new devices, but I've have conversations with other people about supporting other higher-resolution displays (have a browse through some of the issues to see the discourse), and have said before that because we're using a fairly slow interpreted language, as the number of pixels increases exponentially, the longer it will take to render a frame. 

There are some tricks I've employed in other drivers to make it a bit quicker:
* performing a 'diff-to-previous-frame' and only redraw the changed areas, and 
* not drawing black pixels.

If you think about the small monochrome OLEDs - we have to push out 1024 data bytes (= 128 x 64 / 8) for a full frame, but for the color LCD display above (assuming 64k colors) it would need to process 153600 bytes (= 240 x 320 x 2) ... 150 times as much data

If you need quicker rendering times, I would be inclined to use the kernel-based framebuffer-based display drivers that come pre-bundled with the Raspberry Pi distros.

That said, it is possible to add a driver (of course), ... but it will be slow

    >>> import bitstruct
    >>> bitstruct.unpack('u7u6u3', bitstruct.byteswap("2", bytearray([0xfa, 0x01])))

I know you said to delete/erase, but there are some useful things in here for you:

If you need something to be unique together, see the documentation on `unique_together` in the docs: https://josiahcarlson.github.io/rom/model.html

If you need something calculated immediately before data insert/update, override `_before_insert` and/or `_before_update`. I use these when I need to keep an `updated_at` column for tracking changes, as well as generating "checksum" versions for uniqueness (hash the data instead of using the data itself, depending on data sizes).

To generating a mapping of unique 3-tuples to auto-generated numbers (maybe not exactly what you seemed to be after):

```
class MyModel(rom.Model):
    counter = rom.PrimaryKey()

    dim3 = Integer(required=True)
    dim2 = Integer(required=True)
    dim1 = Integer(required=True)

    unique_together = [('dim3', 'dim2', 'dim1')]

    def __repr__(self):
        return f'OBJ{self.id} ({self.dim3}, {self.dim2}, {self.dim1}) = {self.counter}'
```

Note that in this example, you can't change `counter`, it's just the value for this instance. 

If you are looking for something that allows you to directly change data in columns, rom doesn't support that internally, partly due to data consistency issues relating to indexes.

Yes, you should convert the healpix pixels to Ra/Dec values.  But be careful about it, since if you just take the central points of each pixel you can indeed get spurious effects in the 2pt correlation because of grid effects.  The usual trick that people use to avoid this is to spread the data among random positions within the healpix pixel.

For instance, for counts data, you can divide the total N points for a pixel by 10 and select 10 random points within the pixel.  For scalar data (e.g. kappa or shear), you can just take the mean value, but place it at 10 random locations in the pixel.

Depending on the size of your catalog, you might be able to get away with just keeping one point per healpix pixel, but choose the single location at random from within the pixel.  That should remove the dominant pixelization effects, but might still show spurious effects at small separation.

Here are two simple examples, using [aioredis](https://github.com/aio-libs/aioredis) in a more "explicit way" (so to speak) than the redis usage in your examples (as quoted in #39):

```python
async def store_refresh_token(user_id, refresh_token, *args, **kwargs):
    key = 'refresh_token_{user_id}'.format(user_id=user_id)
    async with app.redis_pool.get() as redis:
        await redis.set(key, refresh_token)


async def retrieve_refresh_token(user_id, *args, **kwargs):
    key = 'refresh_token_{user_id}'.format(user_id=user_id)
    async with app.redis_pool.get() as redis:
        return await redis.get(key)
```

I think this is a good improvement, however there is a bug in _search_node(). 'absoluteDistance' needs to be squared before comparing it to 'maxDist', otherwise you end up searching the whole tree.

As it stands, @cyberjoac 's current kdtree.search_knn() outperforms this one by only a slim margin. I think this means the current search_knn() is also searching the whole tree unintentionally (and also sorting the best k repeatedly, which is bad for large k). Square 'absoluteDistance' in @cyberjoac 's _search_node, and search time decreases from (I think) N to log_N - a very nice speedup.

The problem I ran into with `self.py3.register_function('urgent_function', self._urgent_function)` is that it's not stable. It fires off `True` then `False` afterward for as long as it takes... but still painted urgent somewhere... so it's good to have this if you want to be notified immediately instead of cycling until it hits an `urgent` module before it notifies you.

With this branch, I keep a history of all urgent boolean and keep rechecking as it rotates... allowing `format`, `format_closed` to be consistent when opening / closing.. so you can still scroll around... then closes... (still urgent)... meaning there is still an urgent module somewhere... instead of... urgent, open, close, (urgent gone), open... still an urgent module somewhere... or even right in front of you. It's now flawless with this.

*Windows* treats *AltGr* as *Ctrl* + *Alt*, so you will not reliably be able to achieve what you want.

Additionally, [`pynput.keyboard.Controller.alt_gt_pressed`](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/keyboard/_base.py#L492) does not do what you expect; it merely modifies the internal controller state.

My suggestion is to keep record of the keys you are interested in---for example using a `set` where you add *Control* and *alt* when they are pressed and remove them when they are released---and invoke your action when *P* is pressed and all modifiers active.

Dear Filip, dear bumpversion community,

it [just occurred](https://github.com/hiveeyes/phenodata/commit/c4fc58ab) again: _bumpversion_ tried to upgrade from `docopt==0.6.2` to `docopt==0.6.3` when just attempting to bump our own [phenodata](https://github.com/hiveeyes/phenodata) module from 0.6.2 to 0.6.3.

This happens despite having configured the respective [.bumpversion.cfg](https://github.com/hiveeyes/phenodata/blob/0.6.3/.bumpversion.cfg) appropriately - at least having believed so.

We did some minor investigations and want to share our outcome.

1. Configuring the `"search"` option like seen in the `.bumpversion.cfg` referenced above **does not work**. The first line after "search = " **must not be empty**, you have to do it that way:

        search = version='{current_version}'
                 __version__ = '{current_version}'
                 version = u'{current_version}'
                 release = u'{current_version}'

    This is on us.

2. After fixing this, it still didn't work. Bummer! We took the chance to have a look under the hood of the `def replace` workhorse. So:

    - We didn't find appropriate code being capable of replacing **multiple** distinct search expressions at [L243](https://github.com/peritus/bumpversion/blob/v0.5.3/bumpversion/__init__.py#L243) at all. Obviously, the current code will not find any replacements when configured with the search patterns listed above as they are treated as a single opaque string and used as a full search expression.

    - Things start going haywire after checking no replacement has taken place at [L249](https://github.com/peritus/bumpversion/blob/v0.5.3/bumpversion/__init__.py#L249), where _bumpversion_ is running a second replacement attempt presumably as a fallback. Here, just `current_version.original` is used as the search string, so _bumpversion_ actually will perform the replacements as if nothing special has been configured at all. This essentially renders the feature useless or even slightly dangerous.

Please bear with us if we got something wrong when reading _bumpversions_ code. Maybe we missed the place where everything is happening correctly and we just have misconfigured our instance. Otherwise, we will be happy to help getting this issue finally right.

While the world definitvely will keep on spinning anyway, we would be happy if you see any slight chance to improve the situation as we are regularly running into this. Through their subtle nature and the fact packaging usually runs after CI, anomalies like these have a reasonable chance of making it beyond QA and everyone feels sad about having published broken eggs ;].

Cheers,
Andreas.

Hello,

currently there is no dedicated method for this and _Signal_ object returned by the _get_ method does not have the source information.

What you can do now, if you know the channel's group and index (for example using the method _whereis_ , see http://asammdf.readthedocs.io/en/master/api.html)and you have opened the file with the options _full_ or _low_, it to access the source information block:

```python
source = mdf.group[group_index]['channel_sources'][channel_index]
```
Granted this is does not look so nice.

If you use the option _minimum_ then you have to jump another hurdle to get the source, and it get a bit uglier 

```python
import asammdf
source_address = mdf.group[group_index]['channel_sources'][channel_index]
stream = mdf._file
source = asammdf.v4_blocks.SourceInformation(
    address=source_address,
    stream=stream,
)
```

I think the best way to handle this in the future is to add this information to the _Signal_ object returned by the _get_ method.

In the meantime, here is a workaround:

```
import chess
import chess.uci


engine = chess.uci.popen_engine("stockfish")
engine.uci()

class MyInfoHandler(chess.uci.InfoHandler):
    def score(self, cp, mate, lowerbound, upperbound):
        if not lowerbound and not upperbound:
            super(MyInfoHandler, self).score(cp, mate, lowerbound, upperbound)


info_handler = MyInfoHandler()
engine.info_handlers.append(info_handler)

engine.go(movetime=2000)

print(info_handler.info["score"])
```

I'm concerned that using just `BetamaxError` will be not very user friendly. So I think I want us to set up an exception hierarchy here:

- `ValidationError` would inherit from `BetamaxError`
  - Then you could have `InvalidOption` inherit from `ValidationError` so we raise this if the key is not a valid configuration option (L88 in your diff of `betamax/options.py`)
  - And a dictionary of exceptions that map from key to exception, e.g., `match_requests_on` to `MatchersValidationError`. We then get and raise an exception specific to what we're validating.

How does that sound to you?

> The dependency issue arises around shared packages or libraries on which several other packages have dependencies but where they depend on different and incompatible versions of the shared packages. Hi @julianaus.  I think I see the problem.  You need to install pandoc-fignos as root.  On a Mac that means using:

~~~
$ sudo pip install pandoc-fignos
~~~

To upgrade, use

~~~
$ sudo pip install --upgrade pandoc-fignos
~~~

You will need to enter your administrator password when asked.  I will make the documentation more clear on this point.

Once pandoc-fignos is installed, you can test for it in Terminal as follows:

~~~
$ which pandoc-fignos
/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/bin/pandoc-fignos
~~~

That's for my Mac; you will see something different.  If `which` gives nothing, then there is a problem.

Please let me know if this works for you.

In answer to your other question: Yes, you can still run pandoc in Terminal.  Once pandoc-fignos is installed, the language becomes irrelevant.  It should just work.

Reproducer script:

```python
# file: reproducer.py
import gc
import time
from pympler import summary
from pympler import muppy

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed


def random_data(*args):
    time.sleep(1)
    return bytearray(1024*1024*2)


def gen_executor0():
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(1) as executor:
        futures = [executor.submit(random_data) for x in range(5)]
        for future in as_completed(futures):
            futures.remove(future)
            future = None
            yield

def gen_executor1():
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(1) as executor:
        for y in executor.map(random_data, range(5)):
            y = None
            yield 

def main(gen_executor):
    for cycle in gen_executor():
        gc.collect()
        summary.print_(summary.summarize(muppy.get_objects()), limit=4)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print("as_completed()")
    main(gen_executor0)
    print("executor.map()")
    main(gen_executor1)
```

Yeah, the trick is not going to be as simple as you thought, but you are in the correct path. What you need now is:

1. Define your own ``UserSocialAuth`` model, make it inherit from your custom mixin
2. Use that model in the configuration for ``SocialAuthStorageOverride``

```python

from django.db import models
from social.apps.django_app.default.models import Nonce, Association, Code, DjangoStorage


class UserSocialAuth(models.Model, SocialAuthUserMixinOverride):
    class Meta:
        db_table = 'social_auth_usersocialauth'


class SocialAuthStorageOverride(DjangoStorage):
    user = UserSocialAuth
```

You can avoid the mixin and directly define the overridden methods in the custom ``UserSocialAuth`` class.

Ok, I actually got this working now. It was a bit convoluted.

First, the due date is stored in the [`PidLidTaskDueDate`](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/cc839641.aspx) property. The property ID is `0x00008105` and the property set is `PSETID_Task`. But EWS wants the UUID for `PSETID_Task`, so we look that up in the [MS-OXPROPS](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/cc433490(v=exchg.80).aspx) pdf. The UUID is `00062003-0000-0000-C000-000000000046`. Finally, we need to un-comment the `SystemTime` property type in the code, so we can use it. See the commit ba71e21. exchangelib will automatically de-serialize it to an `EWSDateTime`.

In conclusion, the definition for the due date becomes:

```python
class FlagDue(ExtendedProperty):
    property_set_id = '00062003-0000-0000-C000-000000000046'
    property_id = 0x8105
    property_type = 'SystemTime'

Message.register('flag_due', FlagDue)

for i in a.inbox.all():
    print(i.flag_due)
```

There's no built-in tools for migrating old revisions to new model schema,
unfortunately. Even if there were, it would be an extremely slow process,
involving rewriting huge amounts of textual data from the database.

You can do it manually by looping over the relevant Version objects,
parsing the serialized_data property, modifying it, then re-saving the
Version.

You are entirely correct, @owntheweb — the issue you ran across explains the problem.

Per Kepler and Newton, orbits are ellipses in space, so your track and 3D projection are telling the truth — that if you stood back from the Earth and just watched the orbit, it would go in a rough ellipse (which would not be perfect because of the Moon and Sun and Jupiter and other gravitational effects, but would still be close).

But the Earth, as you watched, would be rotating on its axis — so each time the satellite came back to roughly the same point in its orbit, a different continent or city or ocean will have moved under that spot. So even though the satellite keeps reaching the ”same place” in space, it keeps finding a different part of the Earth beneath it.

My Skyfield astronomy package includes some routines that convert the SGP4 coordinates that are relative to the stars into ITRF coordinates that reflect what part of Earth the satellite is over:

https://github.com/brandon-rhodes/python-skyfield/blob/master/skyfield/sgp4lib.py

I have been wondering whether I should move these functions — now that they are written — over into the `sgp4` package itself, which is where they probably belong, since they are peculiar to, and work with, only SGP4 coordinates.

My reluctance has been because they (as currently written, at least) require NumPy arrays to do their matrix rotation, and I have not wanted the `sgp4` package to require outside libraries so that people can more easily use it in their projects. I wonder if I could somehow make the routines work without NumPy if someone did not have it installed?

django-pyodbc-azure 2.0.6 is incompatible with Django 2.0.4 or earlier because it relies on the codes introduced from Django 2.0.5 such as the following.
https://github.com/django/django/blob/2.0.5/django/db/backends/base/schema.py#L17-L30

Basically django-pyodbc-azure has depenency on the latest version of Django at the time of its publication.
A new version of Django usually contains security fixes and Django developers encourage users to upgrade their Django installation to the latest one as soon as possible like this. 
https://www.djangoproject.com/weblog/2018/aug/01/security-releases/

django-pyodbc-azure is also tested only on the latest secure version of Django complying with recommendations like above ones and I don't bother to make sure that the latest django-pyodbc-azure works well with old insecure versions of Django. That's why django-pyodbc-azure requires the latest versions of Django as of its release date.

Hello,
I was facing the same thing, so I did the following changes to the /websocket-server/websocket_server.py file :
Change in WebSocketHandler class:
```python
def read_http_headers(self):
    headers = {}
    # first line should be HTTP GET
    http_get = self.rfile.readline().decode().strip()
        # Next three lines will get your end_point
        global with_end_point
        if re.search('\/([A-Za-z0-9]+)', http_get).group(1):
            with_end_point = re.search('\/([A-Za-z0-9]+)', http_get).group(1)
        assert http_get.upper().startswith('GET')
        # remaining should be headers
        while True:
            header = self.rfile.readline().decode().strip()
            if not header:
                break
            head, value = header.split(':', 1)
            headers[head.lower().strip()] = value.strip()
        return headers
```

Change in WebsocketServer class:
```python
def _new_client_(self, handler):
        self.id_counter += 1
        # This if-else block will check if you have provided an end_point and if you have, it assigns that    
        # endpoint as the 'Id' of the socket connection, otherwise it will assign a number as 'Id'
        if with_end_point:
            client = {
                'id': with_end_point,
                'handler': handler,
                'address': handler.client_address
            }
        else:
            client = {
                'id': self.id_counter,
                'handler': handler,
                'address': handler.client_address
            }
        self.clients.append(client)
        self.new_client(client, self)
```
And finally in your main script, use sever.send_message method to send the message to specific id or end point.
Hope this helps.

Hi Enrique,

What's going on here is that your CSS folks are not in the same
"directory". If you were to run collectstatic, your files would be:

- css/
    main.scss
- myapp/
    css/
        myfile.scss

If you want to import "myfile.scss" you need to specify the full path.
Alternatively, you could reorganize your directory structure (not that I'm
recommending this):

project > static > css > main.scss
myapp > static > css > myapp > myfile.scss

This way you could import "myapp/myfile.scss".

Does this help?

Thanks for the question. For MinPy project, our initial design goals include xx parts: (1) dynamic autograd; (2) fully-compatible numpy support; (3) transparent integration with MXNet's symbolic programming so one-style-achieves-all. During the process, we learned three things:

* We initially intend to release MinPy as a separate package, but it seems that dynamic autograd is so popular (look at pytorch) that MXNet requires this feature very urgently. Therefore, we decide to merge the autograd feature into MXNet (see https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/contrib/autograd.py) by @ZihengJiang .
* A good DNN model builder and an easy-to-use toolchain (initializer, updater, etc.) is crucial for fast development. MinPy's NN module is based on CS231n, which is not sophisticated enough for researches. Therefore, we spend a lot of efforts on a better model builder structure ( @piiswrong @sneakerkg and many others). The new package is developed directly in MXNet (also called NN) and now becomes Gluon.
* Speed is a dominating factor for a DL framework. We found writing fine-grained operators in python yields many efficiency issues. Therefore, the MinPy team is focusing on Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation to improve the efficiency (please stay tuned!).

**TL;DR**: Gluon (successor of NN package) will be our standard DNN model & solver package. MinPy will evolve more into two directions: (1) JIT functionality that can also benefit Gluon and other MXNet packages; (2) More compatible support for NumPy operators.

The storage format can be formalized as a vector. Each element is a pair contains "dim" and "dense/sparse".
For example:
* Row-major equals to [<0, dense>, <1, dense>]
* Col-major equals to [<1, dense>, <0, dense>]
* CSR equals to [<0, dense>, <1, sparse>]
* CSC equals to [<1, dense>, <0, sparse>]

If having more tensor dimensions (e.g. convolution),
* BCSR equals to [<0, dense>, <1, sparse>, <2, dense>, <3, dense>]

I found most variants of current sparse tensor formats can be formalized as this. The paper talks about coordinate, ellpack and dia formats in the future work, which they believe can also be supported.

In terms of implementation, we could change the enum to the vector above, define some common formats (like CSR, CSC), and define the "==" operator. This should simulate the behavior of enum type.

@feng-tao are you referring to [this line](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/pull/3197/files#diff-5aeaca39c3e310ff9883f8ac4d506d1dR141) syncing roles? From my limited understanding of front end stuff, the app actually serving traffic is [the gunicron workers](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/bin/cli.py#L882). After the subprocess are created, [cached_app() will be called to create one app per subprocess](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/bin/cli.py#L832). Since security_manager.sync_roles() lives in create_app(), we should be able to resync the roles/perms everytime we restart the subprocess( not the webserver). Meanwhile, [the workers will be restarted every web_server_worker_timeout](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/bin/cli.py#L812), thus we can refresh both DagBag and roles/perms w/o restarting the master webserver process.

If what I stated is just misunderstanding, I would still strongly suggest we find a way to remove this line--either extract only the needed logic or start the subprocess earlier or something like that. The current approach scales very bad and does not make any sense to me( we're waiting for 2 complete app constructions including 2 single process DagBag parsing before the webserver can serve traffic, at least with the current scale of Airbnb means ~28mins to start a webserver--for around 2k DAG files including some complicated framework DAG files).

In the usage of both of these objects, `poll` is the only method that is not shared between `psutil.Process`, `psutil.Popen`, and `subprocess.Popen`. I cannot find a common function for this purpose between `psutil.Process` and `subprocess.Popen`.

`psutil.Popen` is a convenience wrapper around `subprocess.Popen` that adds methods provided by `psutil.Process`.

As an alternative, in this case:

```python
gunicorn_master_proc = psutil.Popen(run_args, close_fds=True)
```

could have been written as:

```python
gunicorn_master_subproc = psutil.Popen(run_args, close_fds=True)
gunicorn_master_proc = psutil.Process(gunicorn_master_subproc.pid)
```

but that seems needlessly verbose.

I support the idea of migrating `subprocess.Popen` calls to `psutil.Popen` but feel that would be a larger undertaking and would prefer to do that as more of a search/replace separate from functional changes like this `poll` -> `wait` change.

I'm thinking of something like this, where we just use the normal import syntax and parse it :

```python
import ast
import six


def parse_import_line(import_line):
    if not isinstance(import_line, six.string_types):
        raise Exception("Argument must be a string type")
    node = ast.parse(import_line)
    if len(node.body) != 1:
        raise Exception("Invalid import line. Please only specify exactly one import.")
    node = node.body[0]
    if not isinstance(node, (ast.Import, ast.ImportFrom)):
        raise Exception("Only specify import statements")
    from_module = getattr(node, 'module', None)
    name = node.names[0].name
    as_name = node.names[0].asname

    return (from_module, name, as_name)


print parse_import_line("import os")
```

To impersonate another user one can pass `configuration` parameter to the `HiveServer2Connection.cursor()` method:

```python
from impala.dbapi import connect

impersonation_configuration = {
	'hive.server2.proxy.user': 'impersonated_user',  # for HiveServer2
	'impala.doas.user': 'impersonated_user'  # for Impala
}

conn = connect(
    host='hive.server.host.fqdn',
    port=server_port,
    auth_mechanism='GSSAPI',
    kerberos_service_name='hive',
    database='db_name'
)
cursor = conn.cursor(configuration=impersonation_configuration)
cursor.execute('query to execute')
```

The user connecting to the cluster (one that is authenticated using Kerberos) must be allowed to impersonate other users in the cluster configuration.

Hey (I don't work for discogs but I am bored today),

Just taking a peak at your changes here, it looks like you are sort of on the right track, but if you take a look at the return values from the fetch [method][1] it only returns a status code and the content.

This is why the travis CI checks have failed. You are expecting a return value which does not exist. Additionally, as documented [here][2] the rate limit information is returned in the response's headers. So in order to access this information the fetch method will need to either return `resp.info()` or it may need to parse the headers and return the desired rate limit data.

This is because the fetchers do not return the whole response object but rather, information from the response that is relevant to the request. A suggestion I might make (in addition to changing the fetcher to give us rate limit info) would be to add attributes to the client object which is updated by the _get method.

[1]: https://github.com/discogs/discogs_client/blob/master/discogs_client/fetchers.py#L64
[2]: https://www.discogs.com/developers/#page:home,header:home-rate-limiting

I talked to you about this, but wanted to capture the conversation. Lets only do unsigned payload for ``PutObject`` and ``UploadPart`` for the following reasons:
1) The behavior change will only be scoped to ``PutObject`` and ``UploadPart`` leaving a lot of the control plane operations of S3 untouched.
2) We have knowledge upfront if the payload is streaming by looking at the operation model when we create the context so we won't be hard coding ``PutObject`` and ``UploadPart`` into auth signer if we pass that knowledge to the context and we would probably would want to disable body signing for any future S3 operation that has a streaming body for its input.

`__del__` will only be unpredictable if a comtypes object is in a reference cycle. Ok I like the idea of using Matplotlib (+Pillow) if this is what the users are use to do.

Here is what I propose

- add an utility function to encode the image (ndarray) to a base64 string (plus prefix)

```
# mapboxgl.utils.img_encode

# `img_encode` is a wrapper arround `matplotlib.image.imsave` 
# https://matplotlib.org/api/image_api.html#matplotlib.image.imsave

import base64
from io import BytesIO

def img_encode(arr, **kwargs):
    """Encode ndarray to base64 string image data

    Parameters
    ----------
    arr: ndarray (rows, cols, depth)
    kwargs: passed directly to matplotlib.image.imsave
    """
    sio = BytesIO()
    imsave(sio, arr, **kwargs)
    sio.seek(0)
    img_format = kwargs['format'] if kwargs.get('format') else 'png'
    img_str = base64.b64encode(sio.getvalue()).decode()
    
    return 'data:image/{};base64,{}'.format(img_format, img_str)
```

I think we understand the issue. Here is my proposed solution, if there are any concerns raise them here and we can discuss:

Our current implementation iterates over the indexes and attempts to install each top level package using each index.  It used to fail silently, obviously that doesn't work anymore.  This is problematic for a couple of reasons

1. We have to manually iterate over each package and each index unless it's explicitly stated
2. We don't resolve dependencies across indexes (i.e. if they are not on the same index, or if a dependency is on a private index)

My proposal is this:

1. The first listed source will become the primary index
2. The subsequent sources will become arguments to `EXTRA_INDEX_URL`
3. Perhaps we can add an optional flag that indicates that the location should be treated as a `FIND_LINKS` location rather than an `EXTRA_INDEX_URL` which would enable local wheel repositories and the like

Thoughts?

The proof of concept is likely to go forward with the Python DSL if only because @kennethreitz has already written (most?) of the code to handle it. It's important to stress that this is only a proof of concept though and part of that is going to be hashing out how the idea in general works and how pip integrates with it as well as testing any issues with whatever format we're using.

That being said, I was originally very pro Python DSL, but I've since started wavering more towards the TOML side of things. For two reasons:

* I think the Python DSL is much nicer in complex cases like our examples tend to be, but that the TOML file tends to be more succinct in the simpler, more likely to be common cases. For an example, something that just lists some dependencies.
* I am concerned about the question of "What is Python", as others have pointed out, the definition of what is and isn't valid Python changes between releases. We could *hopefully* narrow this down by heavily restricting ourselves only to syntax that is 2.x/3.x compatible and is unlikely to ever change, but the issue is still there.

A lot has changed in the world of packaging since the original download code existed.

I just went ahead and tested this on my local OSX box and this is the timing that I have:

| options            |  time |
|---------------------------------------------|--------|
| no downloading                              | 1.869s |
| downloading w/ a completely empty pip cache | 2.675s |
| downloading w/ a completely full pip cache  | 2.044s |

Even after running it multiple times, the worst time when downloading, is 3.7s. I'm not too excited about a worst case performance penalty of 2 seconds and a best case of a quarter of a second.

This of course assumes everything goes correctly. I'm confused how you managed to get a six minute creation time, we have a 15 second timeout on all HTTP requests with 5 retries and we're going to be requesting ``/simple/{setuptools,wheel,pip}/``. Which comes out to a grand total of 3-4 minutes if your internet is setup in such a way that you're hitting timeouts instead of immediate errors.

I think trying to get the latest version from the internet is a pretty important thing. There was a number of months where creating virtual environments on Python 3.5 meant you got a virtual environment that couldn't create wheels at all because the version of wheel we shipped wasn't compatible with Python 3.5. In addition I think it's a pretty crummy user experience for people to create a virtual environment, and then on their very first invocation of pip they get a warning message saying their pip is out of date. I suspect this will help us get more people onto newer versions faster, particular in situations like Debian/CentOS etc where people will have an older virtualenv that they got from their OS and having them get onto the latest pip even though they're using that older virtualenv.

I think it's nicer than having people run ``python -m pip install -U pip setuptools wheel`` afterwards because not only is the some manual step that's kind of crummy, but that makes the whole thing take even slower since you still have the download, but now you have an uninstall step and an additional install step slowing things down (not to mention the time it takes to literally type out the command if they're not using something like virtualenvwrapper to call it automatically after the creation of a new virtual environment, something that I've heard numerous people say they've been doing).

As far as whether the default should be to download or if the default should be to use the bundled (we have both flags right now, ``--no-download`` and ``--download``) I think the default should be to download. I think that's going to get more people what they actually want. I think that the successfully working case, the time difference is basically a wash, particularly when fully cached.

That being said, I do think it's really crummy that it's taking you 6 minutes to create a virtual environment due to the network access that is involved now by default. It is my assumption that, for most people, they have internet access at any given time *and* for most of them if they don't have internet access it's a matter of policy (like what you have) not a "well in this situation I just don't have internet access" (such as on a plane). I wonder if it'd be an acceptable solution to just make virtualenv respect a configuration file where you can just drop a no-download = true in it so that on your machine, which doesn't have internet access, it just never attempts to access the internet by default?

Digging a bit on this, I've found a trouble:
PyMongo way is now do the indexes check/creation inside the first call to `GridIn.__flush_data`. 

However it doesn't seems a really good idea for performances given the indexes will be enforced for every `GridIn` object flushed (i.e. for every file uploaded) instead of doing it once per `GridFS` instanciation. I guess the strategy is to assume checking for indexes is cheap and this save trouble with concurrent indexes drop.

Considering TxMongo, this is not optimal either given there would be an indexe check/creation defer for each `GridIn` instance (some of them being even hidden from the user, like when we use `GridFS.put` !).

A solution would be to provide to provide a callback to the `GridIn.__init__` to register and collect into the `GridFS` all the defers. However this feels pretty cumbersome so I'm not sure we should do it or stick with the current behavior...

Hi @zbing3 - please see the `BalanacedConsumer` [example](http://pykafka.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) in the documentation for an illustration of how to use zookeeper with pykafka.

You can use the `zookeeper_connect` kwarg on `BalancedConsumer` to specify a set of zookeeper nodes to which to connect.
```python
balanced_consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
    consumer_group='testgroup',
    auto_commit_enable=True,
    zookeeper_connect='myZkClusterNode1.com:2181,myZkClusterNode2.com:2181/myZkChroot'
)
```
If you're using the `SimpleConsumer`, you actually don't need to use zookeeper at all, since `SimpleConsumer` stores its offset information in kafka. pykafka only requires a zookeeper connection when using the `BalancedConsumer`.

If you have other questions about pykafka usage, you can send them to the pykafka mailing list: pykafka-user%40googlegroups.com

A couple of comments on a few implementation details:

- If you are intending to apply a hard threshold, you need to pass `mode='hard'` to `pywt.threshold`.  The default is soft thresholding.

- It would be nice if rather than being forced to manually specify a threshold, some simple common threshold estimates from the literature (e.g. VisuShrink, BayesShrink, or SUREShrink) could be implemented. 

Also, there are substantial improvements in the upcoming release of `PyWavelets` that could be of use here (the current plan is to release  v0.4.0 on Dec 28). 

 - 2D and nD wavelet transforms are MUCH (up to two orders of magnitude) faster than in v0.3.0.
 - I don't think you will have to worry about the sizing issues anymore.  These should be automatically handled by the `wavedec`*/`waverec`* functions now.
 - There are now n-dimensional versions of the multilevel transforms:  `wavedecn`/`waverecn`,  so this wouldn't have to be restricted to 2D/2D+color images.

Here's a summary of what I've found. 

`skimage` uses the inertia tensor (via it's eigenvalues) to estimate the major and minor axis lengths; this is fairly standard and is also used e.g. in e.g. ImageJ's standard ellipse fitting routine. 
However, this method does (I think) normalize using the image mass (sum), which is what's causing the issue.  

Essentially the problem is that a hollow ring has the same normalized inertia tensor as a filled circle of a larger radius.   

I guess the question comes down to the following - what do we want the ellipse parameters given by `skimage` to reflect? 

My guess would have been something along the lines of, at least for an actual ellipse like shape, the outline of that shape! 

However, due to the complications arising from more complex shapes, maybe the best compromise in the end is the normalized inertia tensor approach after all.  

We could of course add in additional techniques too, and have these as optional outputs. BoneJ (an ImageJ plugin) has several techniques: 
https://github.com/mdoube/BoneJ/blob/master/src/org/doube/geometry/FitEllipsoid.java

Hey @jni, great writeup. I'm really impressed with the way you summarized and explained the values of the project.

Here are my toughts.

### User data + magic
I think maybe the most important step for the 1.0 version will have to be deciding how **care for the users’ data** and respect the **We don’t do magic** go hand in hand with the library being **easy to use**. 

A rule that I am converging to in my own work is: `img_as_*` should never be called in algorithmic functions.

Funny enough, `img_as_*` is imported in the top level `__init__.py` which I think warrants some rethinking. `img_as_*` is particularly useful for certain types of data, mainly those obtained from 8bit cameras, and definitely useful for saving images in common "non-scientific" formats.

### Helping users
I've been personally experimenting with the `stacklevel` parameter of the `warn`. This helps point users to the line in their code that is causing the warning to appear, instead of the line that outputs the warning. I've managed to create pretty fancy decorators for deprecations. I'll share this work with the `scikit-image` team soon to try and get some feedback.

### Metadata + units
I've been experimenting quite a bit with `xarray` and `dask` since the sprint at BIDS (thank you all for pointing me to those two projects). 

`xarray` actually does a good job at keeping things like units organized.
It is also transparent with many numpy operations, but I occasionally hit a few hiccups. 

I expect that sticking to numpy might be a good idea seeing as many people are working toward making sure the [`__array_function__` protocol](http://www.numpy.org/neps/nep-0018-array-function-protocol.html) becomes a reality.

When xarray doesn't work, I often have to access the "data" attribute, or call `np.asarray` which really freaks me out when I'm passing parameters to my low level c-libraries that need to write back to the array.

This problem could be that the code checkout you have is not the same version as the NuPIC you have installed on your system. Try this to find out what version you have:

    python -c 'import pkg_resources;n=pkg_resources.get_distribution("nupic");b=pkg_resources.get_distribution("nupic.bindings");print n.project_name, n.version;print b.project_name, b.version'

This should print out the [versions](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/wiki/How-to-tell-what-version-of-nupic-you-have) you have installed, like this:

    nupic 0.6.0
    nupic.bindings 0.6.0

Use the `X.Y.Z` version you got for NuPIC and checkout the version of the code the matches the installation. From your git checkout, run:

    git fetch upstream

This fetches tags from upstream, where upstream is `git@github.com:numenta/nupic.git`

Then run this to checkout the code specific to your installation:

    git checkout X.Y.Z

Where `X.Y.Z` corresponds to the NuPIC version you got in the first step.

```
module
```

[tripit/slate](https://github.com/tripit/slate)
- Pros
  - Nice documentation, used by a lot of companies.
  - Markdown format even readable in Github
- Cons
  - Very simple markdown format (also an advantage in some way). No syntax to specify error codes, parameters, etc... . This is for really high level doc with lots of examples.

[sphinxcontrib-httpdomain](https://pythonhosted.org/sphinxcontrib-httpdomain/)
- Pros
  - Quite complete syntax: request headers, query parameters, json body parameters...
  - Part of the documentation can be in the code and part outside, avoiding bloating the code with examples. [claimstore example](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/claimstore/blob/master/docs/users.rst)
- Cons
  - It is possible to specify json parameters in the request body but I don't see any way to say that it's also valid for other formats (ex: xml)
  - Output is not as nice visually as tripit/slate.

@lnielsen @jirikuncar @tiborsimko 
Just to be sure, what are the use-cases for this documentation?
1/ one doc per module containing a REST API
2/ helping services generate their own documentation by aggregating the doc from all rest apis registered on an app?

The main constraint is that we know that some modules will have more than just the REST API so we need a sphinx doc for the rest of the code.
Another constraint, if we go for multiple instances of one module api with different endpoints (as proposed here inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest#9), is that we can't use the module documentation "as is" for the service api. The endpoints would not match.
What's more, as some services will have custom behavior depending on record metadata fields, (like we plan to do for B2Share), the service doc would be missing some important parts.

For module documentation I would use sphinxcontrib-httpdomain as we already use sphinx for the rest of the code, and leave services in charge of their own API documentation.

As the markdown format is simple, it might be possible to bootstrap a doc from the sphinx one if it is really needed, that way the endpoints would be valid. This could be done in a third-party module.

I built the wheels locally using the script amended by https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/issues/64 and can confirm a fix.

> I don't understand how this fixes anything. Nothing is looking for -2 or -3 afaict.

The `-2`/`-3` return values are irrelevant for any practical use, because the exception will be raised by the caller.

> Furthermore, TEST_KEY_SET_OR, which calls COMPARE calls PyErr_Occurred to detect the error.  Does your change somehow magically make the test pass? If so, I'm baffled as to how it could.

`PyObject_RichCompareBool` returns `-1` to indicate an error occurred, but the old code ignores that possibility and calls it a second time.  The fix is to avoiding making the second `PyObject_RichCompareBool` call with an error set, which (in Python 3.5) causes a `SystemError`.

> (Your change also forces an unnecessary comparison, in many cases.)

ff4c330 drops the `Py_EQ` comparison if the `Py_LT` one returns true.

I was writing this because i was playing with this disassembler, and I was
trying to see which things compressed well and which didn't.
You can do a lot with clever block types and well chosen zero out one bit
distance and insert & copy encoding, and sometimes with contexts.
What I found missing is a way to repeat the literal length without having
to reencode the extra bits (with distance you can use last to handle that).
I wrote a small program that produces the shortest compression i could
think of in that case (see my github fork).

From all my playing and struggling, I found two ways to squeeze the
compression to the limit:
Allow for more (flexible??) contexts; make a way to say "same literal
length".
A context based on the character type 8 characters ago, for example, would
solve all problems (apart from creating lots of problems, of course...).

We've made some changes in IPython recently that have slowed down import. We are looking into ways that we can address that. I think it's definitely a good idea to only import IPython if it's actually going to be invoked, rather than unconditionally. I think lazy imports are generally a good idea if you have a fair amount of optional functionality.

As for the dependency, if a minority of users actually use it, making it an optional dependency (or no dependency at all) makes sense to me. You can always have the interactive mode do something like:

```python
try:
    import IPython
except ImportError:
    raise ImportError("IPython is required for interactive mode, `pip install ipython` to get it...")
```

H2O scales vertically with the size (number of rows) of the dataset. Matrix operations (like the Cholesky decomposition) are not distributed. If you have a big problem in terms of the number of variables you will need more memory per node.

As general reminder for people who find this issue: please note that we recommend driver memory to be set the same as the executor memory. The reason is that the driver can actually do some of the non-distributed computation (like the matrix algebra). @axiomoixa I would recommend doing the same in your R script as well.

Hi @ClaudiuPID 

1. `bootstrap.dcs` section is written into DCS only once and if you later (for existing cluster) change it in configuration file it wont take any effect.
2. Everything stored in DCS would have the same values on all cluster nodes. I.e. if DCS contains `/var/lib/postgresql/data/sql1/` that means all it would be the same in archive_command on all cluster nodes.

If you need to change some configuration parameters in DCS, you have to use [REST API](http://patroni.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dynamic_configuration.html#rest-api)

And if you want to have a different archive_command on the nodes, you need to set it's value in the configuration file on every node. This way it will work as on v0.9.
```yaml
postgresql:
  parameters:
    archive_command: 'mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/wal_archive && test ! -f /var/lib/postgresql/wal_archive/%f && cp /var/lib/postgresql/data/sqlX/%p /var/lib/postgresql/wal_archive/%f'
```

And probably remove archive_command from DCS config:
```bash
$ curl -s -XPATCH -d \
        '{"postgresql":{"parameters":{"archive_command":null}}}' \
        http://localhost:8008/config
```

P.S. your archive_command is broken. cp command is not doing fsync, so there is no guaranty that data was really written on disk.

Is it really a problem to set a correct `PATH` before starting up Patroni?

```bash
PATH=/usr/lib/postgresql/9.5/bin:$PATH patroni ....
```

 If `pg_ctl` (or any other binary) can be found multiple time it will choose the first one:
```bash
$ PATH=/usr/lib/postgresql/9.3/bin:/usr/lib/postgresql/9.5/bin:$PATH pg_ctl -V
pg_ctl (PostgreSQL) 9.3.13
$ PATH=/usr/lib/postgresql/9.5/bin:/usr/lib/postgresql/9.3/bin:$PATH pg_ctl -V

```

I don't know how clean it will be; we're not very good about consolidating where we access username/password information for the database credentials.  I suspect that there will be some debugging involved here.

Also, I don't think "encrypyted-value" is a good key, because you're not supplying a value, your supplying a key.  Note that Kubernetes secrets are supplied unencrypted inside the container, but that's not a serious objection to the name.

An alternative "zeroconf" version of this is to have preset key names for the shared secrets implementations.  For example, if we required that all of the kubernetes secrets have specfic names, all the user would need to supply is the secrets filename and location.  That would definitiely be more user-friendly, but might not map well to other solutions.

The particular plan did not work out (*): after playing with the standby mode flag we've decided the interactions with the standby mode flag and the decision on when to clear this flag  (given that the "primary standby" node may just crash or shut down) were too complex to manage them automatically, so instead, we would rely on a client to set the keys in DCS in order for the existing cluster to point elsewhere, and clear those keys when we are done. The user setting up the external master would also be responsible for creating replication slots or setting up WAL archiving.

Of course, we need the client support and documented process on how to set things up, which will be a part of this task.

> Avoid deleting replication slots that were not created by Patroni, leaving the task of creating actual slots to the user. This is suboptimal since replication slots itself are not replicated and would have to be created anew after each promotion.

This seems like a cleaner approach to me though.  This is a limitation in Postgres at the time, and trying to have Patroni work around that seems less than optimal.

When the replication slot gets recreated on another member, it will have lost the place the former slot was on, and the end user will possibly/probably need to take special action to resync anyway.

Hello, Im wrote some simple function for download attachments

```
def download_atachment(issueid):
	filename = "attachments/" + issueid + ".zip"
	fp = open(filename, "wb")
	curl = pycurl.Curl()
	curl.setopt(pycurl.URL, "https://example.jira.com/secure/attachmentzip/" + issueid + ".zip")
	curl.setopt(pycurl.USERPWD, "jirauser:jirapassword") 
	curl.setopt(pycurl.WRITEDATA, fp)
	curl.setopt(pycurl.SSL_VERIFYHOST, 0)
	curl.setopt(pycurl.SSL_VERIFYPEER, 0)
	curl.perform()
	curl.close()
	filename_size = os.path.getsize(filename)
	if filename_size == 0:
		os.remove(filename)
		return None
	else:
		return filename
```

Hi @qcaron,

Unfortunately django-cookie-law is not tested using Django 1.7 at the moment, so you'd have to work out way to handle this:
https://github.com/TyMaszWeb/django-cookie-law/blob/master/.travis.yml#L7

From your traceback it seems like you're using the `{% cookielaw_banner %}` tag in your `404.html` template (or the one `404.html` is extending).

There are a few issues here:

* you should rather not allow Django to serve your `favicon.ico`
* you should make your 404.html as simple as possible so you don't get further errors when for example the database dies
* `django-cookie-law` should rather raise a warning than an exception when request context is not found in the response.

It'd be great if you could take care of the last point and open a PR. Otherwise I'll try to look into this in the upcoming week.

I also noticed that and changed it to `sys.maxsize` in PR #7 ([`sys.maxint` has been removed in Python 3.1](https://docs.python.org/3.1/whatsnew/3.0.html#integers) since there is no longer a limit to the value of integers).

Note that, though WinKerberos is written using SSPI, it fully supports authenticating to a krb5 KDC and authenticating to a service that uses krb5. It also supports almost all of the client side features of PyKerberos. How multiple user credentials are handled is one major structural difference between SSPI and krb5 that I can't hide or workaround.

I got a response from Julien Simon on youtube about this topic, I paste it here:

Hi Manel, thank you for your question. Spark and Spark ML are great tools, more power to both :) Still, I see a few reasons why combining them with Sagemaker would make sense:

1) Decoupling ETL infrastructure from training infrastructure. They could have different sizing requirements (compute and/or storage) and you wouldn't want to oversize your Spark cluster just because one part of the overall process requires more capacity. Sure, you can resize an EMR cluster dynamically, but it's extra work and potentially troublesome if you have a lot of data. SageMaker will start and terminate instances automatically, which I find cleaner and simpler :)

2) In the same vein, imagine you need GPUs for training. It would be costly and sub-optimal to run your EMR cluster on GPU instances just for this.

3) Spark ML is great and has indeed a lot of algos. SageMaker is just getting started and we'll keep adding scalable implementions of state-of-the-art algos, so maybe one of them will actually catch your eye, such as DeepAR (released a couple of days ago) https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/now-available-in-amazon-sagemaker-deepar-algorithm-for-more-accurate-time-series-forecasting/

4) Deploying models in production on managed infrastructure. To me, this is the single most important feature in SageMaker: call a single API and deploy an endpoint. 

You've certainly seen this repo, it has a number of examples: https://github.com/aws/sagemaker-spark

Hope this helps. Have fun :)﻿

For `set_dropout_mask`
"If this function is not called on batched input, the same mask will be applied across all batch elements. Use this to apply different masks to each batch element"

Hi all,

I am a very strong supporter of a github based approach using structured
text files (one can still have a database backend working in sync with the
text files to get the best of both worlds).
This is in my opinion the only way to get a community curated model and
community curation on models.
Otherwise it is very complicated to get changes/fixes into models. See for
instance RECON3D where I found some errors but there is no clear platform
for reporting this issues or even fixing this.
With a text based github approach this fixes could be immediatly available
to the community via a pull request

I would love to fix things like the following reported issues in RECON3D
https://github.com/SBRG/bigg_models/issues/291
https://github.com/SBRG/bigg_models/issues/290
https://github.com/SBRG/bigg_models/issues/287

After more than 20 days of reporting the first issue of a incorrect gene in
RECON3D these are still not fixed in the underlying database
http://vmh.uni.lu/#reaction/HDCA24Gtr
Whereas in text file via pull request everybody could be immediatly working
with an updated Recon v3.02.

The only way to get the community to work together and use the curation
potential of all scientist working with models is a fully open easy to
update format.
Github with structured text file is an established and working approach.

I really hope the community is working into this direction instead of
hiding information in in-house databases.

Best Matthias

Re: object resource having encryption key metadata but not key materials:  It is a little bit confusing in the first glance, as object resources can appear in both requests and responses.  But if you look more closely, the table denotes which fields are "writable".  Only those fields can be updated.  Key metadata cannot be updated, they are only helpful in responses.

Re: why the keys are passed in as headers rather than the post body:  Actually adding the keys to the request body is not as straightforward as it seems.  In our current API, a request body either contains object data or object metadata.  And key materials are neither.

@elibixby `google.cloud.pubsub` has implemented `get_iam_policy`, `set_iam_policy`, and `test_iam_permissions` for two years.  PR #3188  is only factoring out the "shared" bits of `google.cloud.pubsub.iam` into a new `google.cloud.iam` module.  I have updated it to address your issues concerning custom roles.

The bits of your proposal which add a common subclass for the implementation of those API methods are trickier to address:  the endpoints are not consistent across resource types:  `storage` appends `/iam` and uses `GET` / `PUT`, while `pubsub` uses `GET` against '<resource path>:getIamPolicy` / `POST` against `<resource path>:setIamPolicy`.  The implementations are so minimal that I plan to just fork them, rather than attempting to framework away those differences.

@JustinLovinger @DavidLP:  I'm not seeing this error on MacOS.  However, do I see that at least @JustinLovinger is using Windows.  On Windows, you are missing `freeze_support` -- which is required on window to be able to run from `__main__`.  It's not a bug, it's a requirement inherited from `multiprocessing`.

Try:
```
if __name__ == '__main__':
    pathos.helpers.freeze_support()
    pool = pathos.multiprocessing.Pool(processes=2)
    print(pool.map(bar, [0, 1]))
```

Let me know if this fixes your code, (and close the ticket)... or if it doesn't please let me know what you are seeing that's different.
